items.q: Why are food chains usually limited to about 4 or 5 trophic levels?
items.correct: Only ~10 percent of energy is transferred between trophic levels; eventually too little energy remains to support another level.
items.misconception: There aren't enough types of animals to fill more trophic levels.
items.random_wrong: Larger animals always eat the next ones up, and there aren't enough giant animals.
items.why: Students don't grasp the energy-loss aspect of food chains; they think the limit is biological diversity rather than thermodynamic efficiency.

items.correct: No — humans and chimpanzees share a common ancestor; both have evolved from it.
items.misconception: Humans evolved directly from chimpanzees, which is why chimps still exist as a less-evolved version.
items.random_wrong: Humans evolved from monkeys but chimps evolved from gorillas.
items.why: The phrase 'descended from apes' and linear evolution diagrams encourage 'A turned into B' thinking, missing the branching tree model.

items.q: How do insulin and glucagon work together to regulate blood glucose?
items.correct: They are antagonistic — insulin lowers blood glucose (cells take up glucose, store as glycogen); glucagon raises it (glycogen broken back down to glucose).
items.misconception: Insulin and glucagon both lower blood glucose using different mechanisms.
items.random_wrong: Insulin works on muscles and glucagon works on the brain.
items.why: Students don't grasp 'antagonistic hormones' — they often think both hormones do similar jobs because both are about glucose.
The answer examiners want is: Oxygen loss in water. “Algae outcompeting weeds” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: Clean air. “Polluted water” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Atmospheric CO₂ in ppm is roughly:
items.why: Exact answer: 420. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
